City of Alameda California

March 20, 1992

Jill Stecher
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814-0807

Re: Additional Information for Request Number 91-087

Dear Ms. Stecher:

Enclosed please find the additional information we discussed:

1. A chart showing the projects of the Economic Development
Commission which were forwarded to the City Council for
action. This chart shows the recommendation of the
Economic Development Commission and the action of the
City Council.

2. A copy of a letter from an attorney discussing possible
Government Code section 1090 implications. This letter
may provided you with some additional background
information. City staff believes, at this point, that
the Economic Development Commission's involvement will be
limited to reviewing the plans and making recommendations
about the overall Civic Center Plans.

For your information, the Economic Development Commission is
currently listed as an advisory body in the City's Conflict of
Interest Code.

Please let me know if I can provide you with any additional
information. We would appreciate your response as soon as
possible.

Very truly yours,

Heather C. McLaughlin
Assistant City Attorney

Enclosures

THE LAW
OFFICES OF
ROBERT
J. LOGAN
152 NORTH THIRD STREET
SUITE 201 · SAN JOSE, CA 95112
TELEPHONE · 408.287.2156
FACSIMILE · 408.280.1749

March 13, 1992

Ms. Carol A. Korade
City Attorney
City of Alameda
2263 Santa Clara Avenue
Alameda, CA 94501

Dear Ms. Korade:

You asked this office to provide an analysis regarding implication of
section 1090 of the Government Code as it relates to the activities of
Robert Wood, a member of the City's Economic Development Committee
and a local architect.

FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The facts giving rise to this request are as follows. The City is
interested in making renovations to the City Hall and is also considering
possible development scenarios for a new Civic Center project (collectively
referred to as the "Projects"). The Projects would involve the preparation
and review of a specific plan. The specific plan process may be reviewed
by the City's Economic Development Commission (the "Commission"). Robert
Wood is a member of the Commission.

Mr. Wood has approached the City Manager to express an interest
in performing architectural services for the Projects. Mr. Wood's
membership on the Commission could result in his participation in the
review of the Projects. It is not clear that the Ordinance creating the
Commission (Ordinance No. 2489) includes the duty to review specific
plans, but for purposes of this opinion it is assumed that the Commission
would review the Projects and upon completion of the review, the
Commission would make recommendations to the City Council regarding
the specific plan for the Projects.

QUESTION PRESENTED

If Mr. Wood participates in the review process and is subsequently
successful in obtaining the contract for architectural services, would his
participation result in a violation of section 1090 of the Government Code?

Korade
March 13, 1992
Page 2

SHORT ANSWER

If the Commission's review of the Projects includes negotiation, planning,
drawing of any plans and specifications, or solicitation of bids for
architectural services, a contract with Robert Wood for architectural services
would violate section 1090 of the Government Code.

DISCUSSION

Codification of the common law prohibition regarding self dealing
with respect to contracts is found in Government Code section 1090.¹
Section 1090 basically prohibits a public officer or employee from making
a contract in which he or she is financially interested. A three prong test
is used to determine a violation of section 1090. The person involved must
be a public official, employee or advisor/consultant to a public agency. The
person must participate in the making of a contract. The official in
question must have a financial interest in the contract. To constitute a
violation a contract must be executed.

A violation of section 1090 is not only a crime, but conviction will
result in permanent disqualification from holding any public office in
California (Gov. Code § 1097). Moreover, any contract made in violation
of section 1090 is void and cannot be enforced. An individual involved in
a 1090 violation can also suffer catastrophic forfeitures. (For a good
example see Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633)

The scope of persons subject to section 1090 is very broad. All city
public officials, employees and consultants are subject to section 1090. The
application has also been extended persons employed in advisory capacities
who participate in making the contract through their advisory function.
(Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262
Cal.App.2d 222.) The very essence of section 1090 is predicated upon the
concept that public officials "are expected to exercise absolute loyalty and
undivided allegiance to the best interests of the governmental body or
agency. . . ." Id. at p. 237.

Once it has been established that a public official is involved, the
next issue is whether a contract has been made in his/her official capacity.
The first step of the inquiry is to be certain that a contract has been
finalized. If it is established that a public official with a financial interest
has participated in the making of the contract, a violation occurs.

¹All future code citations will be to the Government Code unless otherwise specifically noted.

Korade
March 13, 1992
Page 3

The courts have defined the making of a contract to include
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning,
drawing of plans and specifications and solicitation of bids. Millbrae
Association for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, supra, 262
Cal.App.2d. 222, 237.

The public official must have a financial interest in the contract
which is being made. Section 1090 does not clearly define what constitutes
a financial interest, although several cases have interpreted the term
broadly. In addition, the Legislature has codified certain remote interests
which are not deemed sufficient to rise to the level of being interested in
the contract. These remote interests are specific and must be disclosed to
the body on which the officer sits and noted in the official records (Gov.
Code § 1091). Often times these "remote" interests disqualify the individual
under the Political Reform Act.

From the facts provided it is not altogether clear what the level of
the Commission's involvement is in the review of the Projects. Therefore,
the certainty of Mr. Wood's participation in the planning for the Projects
or involvement leading up to employment of an architect is speculative.
However, assuming arguendo, Mr. Wood's duties on the Commission include
participation, negotiations, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, or
solicitation of bids, any subsequent actions which would result in his making
an agreement with the City to do the architectural work would violate
section 1090. Applying the three-prong test, Mr. Wood was a member of
the commission, participated in the making and culmination of the contract,
and has a clear financial interest in the contract as he would receive
compensation for professional services.

If Mr. Wood's involvement is restricted to review of schematic or
conceptual plans for the Projects, the potential application of section 1090
is more remote. If the involvement of the Economic Development
Commission, includes recommending approval of the specifications for the
Projects, a reasonable nexus between the participation of Mr. Wood and his
subsequent ability to bid the project as an architect may be established. In
that instance, it is possible a section 1090 violation could occur.

If the Commission only reviews schematics and makes
recommendations on the overall design of the Projects, then I believe
participation does not reach the level of a section 1090 violation. The
second prong of the test, participation, in the making of a contract would
not be satisfied by these circumstances.

Korade
March 13, 1992
Page 4


This opinion does not address other possible violations of law, such
as the Political Reform Act. If you have any questions please do not
hesitate to call.
                                                    Sincerely yours,

                                                    [signature]
                                                    Robert J. Logan

RJL/kl

City of Alameda California

February 11, 1991

General Counsel
Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O.Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Sir or Madam:

     As follow-up to our recent phone conversation, please be
advised of the City of Alameda's request for a letter of legal
advice based on the following facts. The question is one involving
a potential conflict of interest between Alameda commission member
Robert Wood's private architectural business and a project that may
be assigned/contracted by the City to this private business.

     Mr. Wood was a City Planning Board member for 11.5 years
and was the chair of the board for 2 of those years. He is
currently  vice-chairman of the City's Economic Development
Commission (EDC), which is staffed in part by representatives of
the Community Development Department.  Specifically, the EDC
consists of seven members; five of spots on the commission are
earmarked for members of community Development agencies involved
in the areas of banking, retail, real estate, manufacturing, and
marine-related interests. Two spots are filled by the community at
large.  Mr. Wood is one of these at-large members.

     The EDC was created in August 1990 by ordinance for the
purpose of providing planning in the areas of economic
redevelopment and commercial revitalization, and to provide policy
advice to the City Council. (See attached Alameda Ordinance #2489
and Community Development Director memo regarding the EDC).
Regarding the specific activities of the EDC since its inception
in August 1990: it made no recommendations or proposals to 1990.
At its January 1991 meeting it made one recommendation to the
Community Improvement Commission regarding a proposed business and
waterfront improvement plan. The EDC recommendation was to solicit
public review of the plan, which was accepted. Currently, the EDC
is reviewing a project from the city Council and is in the process
of making a recommendation to them regarding it.

Office of the City Attorney, Room 314

City Hall
Santa Clara Avenue at Oak Street · 94501
415.748.4544

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 11, 1991
P. 2

The FPPC previously advised that once there is a history
of a particular advisory board's recommendations being routinely
accepted without amendment or modification, the board does convert
from a solely advisory function to one making a governmental
decision and would be covered by the conflict of interest codes.
(See November 28, 1989 advisory letter from Jeanette E. Turvill to
the Carlsbad City Attorney's office). In addition, I spoke with
Jeanette Turvill of the FPPC who confirmed that the EDC should be
treated as solely advisory at this time, with their status being
reviewed in 12 months.

The City project currently being discussed is at a stage
of asking for architecture proposals to be submitted for an
Alameda Civic Center design. This project was initiated by the
Alameda Planning Board.

As a point of general information, once a proposal is
submitted to the City it is accepted or rejected outright by the
City Manager or their designee (if under $75,000). "Designee" in
this case can be any City department but is usually given to the
department most directly involved or affected by the project. In
this situation that designee will be the City Planning Board. It
is anticipated that the EDC itself would become involved in
reviewing and commenting on the Request For Proposal and the
specific phases of the project, should it advance beyond the
proposal stage.

Mr. Wood is a partner in an Oakland, California
architectural firm that would like to make a proposal on the Civic
Center project.  Specifically, the firm is a privately held
corporation with a total staff of 25, seven of whom are
shareholders and officers. The firm has 3 associates, none of whom
are officers or shareholders.  Mr. Wood's title is Senior Vice
President and he currently holds approximately 10% of the
corporations stock, with the remaining stock being distributed
among the other six officers.  His primary role in the firm is as
a principal in charge of specific design projects for private and
governmental (municipal and other) clients.  The firm has not
previously been involved in any Alameda projects.  If the firm was
granted the project Mr. Wood would be the primary person
responsible for its successful completion. The firm's proposal
would entail preparing planning studies of the existing city of
Alameda Civic Center area and recommendations for possible future
development improvements.

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 11, 1991
P. 3

     In terms of potential financial benefits to the firm, Mr.
Wood states that the company received 4 million dollars in gross
revenues last year, with his personal share of that being the
increase in the value of his ownership position due to any profits
on a net revenue of $2.5 million (gross minus out-going consultant
fees and direct project expenses). He anticipates the proposal for
the Civic Center project to be approximately $23,000.00. This
works out to 6/10th's of 1% of 4 million as to the firm, with Mr.
Wood's interest being 1/10th of the profit on this amount as a
stock benefit. Mr. Wood is a salaried member of the firm and would
not receive additional compensation by acting as principal for the
firm on this City project. However, there is the potential that
he could receive a bonus at the end of the year (as could other
employees), based on the overall profitability of the firm. This
overall profitability could of course be affected by the proposal.

                    Specific Questions for Analysis:

     1.   What, if any, conflict of interest does Mr. Wood have?

     2.   If there is a conflict of interest, does this preclude him
          from submitting a proposal? From reviewing the project as an
          EDC member? What steps and safeguards do you suggest?

     3.   Please confirm the position of the FPPC that the EDC is
          considered an advisory body at this time and their status
          should be reviewed in 12 months to establish a history of
          recommendations.

          Any assistance you can provide in answering these
     questions is appreciated. Please contact me at (415) 748-4544 if
     you need any follow-up information regarding the above facts.


                                        Very Truly Yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Carol A. Korade
                                        City Attorney

CAK/ec

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 11, 1991
P. 4


ATTACHMENTS:
     City of Alameda Ordinance #2489
     Community Development Director memo to the City Council
     regarding the EDC

CC:  City Manager
     Community Development Director
     Economic Development Manager
     Mr. Robert Wood

City of Alameda California

[SEAL/LOGO]

July 17, 1992

Jill Stecher
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814-0807

Re: Additional Information for Request Number 91-087

Dear Ms. Stecher:

Per your request, I have provided the following additional or
updated information:

1. Mr. Wood's firm is called MWM Architects, Inc.

2. Mr. Wood currently holds 16.6% of the corporation's
stock. The dollar value for projects done by his firm
will increase accordingly.

3. Both the Planning Board and Economic Development
Commission ("EDC") are advisory to the City Council.
Each body may in certain instances take final action on
an issue which would only go to City Council if appealed.
However, the EDC only has an advisory role in regards to
the City Hall project or Civic Center Plan.

The Planning Board is responsible for work done by the
typical planning commission. The EDC is responsible for
issues related to redevelopment and other economic
development. Neither the Planning Board nor EDC are
charged with reviewing the other body's decisions; each
body may take in to consideration the other's decisions
when making their own decision.

4. The question requiring immediate attention is whether Mr.
Wood and/or his firm may submit a proposal for the
remodel of City Hall. The City Council has authorized
the preparation of plans to remodel City Hall.
Remodeling will include rearranging the spaces for the
various departments with offices in City Hall, creating
office space in the north floor attic which is currently
unused, making City Hall accessible, and reinforcing City
Hall which is unreinforced masonry.

Office of the City Attorney, Room 314

City Hall
2263 Santa Clara Avenue 94501-4456
Tel: 510.748 4544
Fax: 510.748 4661

JUL 17 '92 16:33 ! 510 748 4691 PAGE.001


07-1792 04:48PM FROM ALAMEDA CITY ATTORNY TO 9-1-916-327-2026 F002/002


Hallabrin
July 17, 1992
Page 2


     5.   The other pressing question is whether Mr. Wood and/or
his firm may submit a proposal for the Civic Center
project.  This project will involve planning for the
existing buildings in the Civic Center area (City Hall,
the Police Station and the Library) and incorporating any
recommendations of the City's consulting firm which is
evaluating the City in terms of planning, design and
economic feasibility of certain projects or areas such as
the Civic Center area.

     6.   The chart previously provided shows "N/A" which stands
for Not Applicable.

     Please let me know if I can provide you with any additional
information.  I have not reviewed the above information with the
staff person of the EDC.  If he has any corrections or additions I
will forward those to you at the beginning of next week.  We would
appreciate your response as soon as possible.

                                        very truly yours,

                                        Heather C. Mc Laughlin
                                        Assistant City Attorney

Enclosures
cc:  Robert Wood
     Economic Development Manager

Dear Ms. Korade:
You asked this office to provide an analysis regarding implication of
section 1090 of the Government Code as it relates to the activities of
Robert Wood, a member of the City's Economic Development Committee
and a local architect.
FACTUAL BACKGROI-IND
The facts giving rise to this request are as follows. The City is
interested in making renovations to the City Hall and is also considering
possible development scenarios for a new Civic Center project (collectively
referred to as the "Projects"). The Projecs would involve the preparation
and review of a specific plan. The specific plan process may be reviewed
by the City's Economic Development Commission (the "Commission").
Robert Wood is a member ef fts Qemmi55i6n.
Mr. Wood has approached the City Manager to express an interest
in performiag architectural services for the Projects. Mr. Wood's
membership on the Commission could result in his participation in the
rwiew of the Projects. It is not clear that the Ordinance creating the
Commission (Ordinance No. 2489) includes the duty to review specific
plans, but for purposes of this opinion it is assumed that the Commission
would review the Projects and upor completion of the review, the
Corrmission would make recommendations to the City Council regarding
the specific plan for the Projects.
OI'ESTION PRESENTED
If Mr. Wood participates in the review process and is subsequently
successful. in obtaining the cotrtract for architectural services, *o,ito tii
participation result in a violation of section 1090 of the Government code?


Korade
March 13, 1992
sections.analysis:
Codification of the corrmon law prohibition regarding self dealing
with respect to contracts is found in Government Code section 10901
Section 1090 basicaily prohibits a public officer or employee from making
a contract in which he or she is financially interested. A three prong test
is used to determine a violation of section 1090. The person involved must
be a public official, employee or advisor/consultant to a public agency. The
persotr must participate in the making of a contract. The official in
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: must have a financial interest in the contract. To constitute a
vioiation a contract must be executed.
A violation of section 1090 is not only a crime, but conviction will
result in permanent disqualiEcation from holding any public office in
Califomia (Gov. Code $ 1097). Moreover, any contract made in violation
of section 1090 is void 41d gannot be enforced. An individual involved in
a 1090 violation can also suffer catastrophic forfeitures. (For a good
sxample see Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633)
The scope of persons subject to sectiotr 1090 is very broad. All city
public officials, employees atrd coDsultants are subject to section 1090. The
application has also been extended persons employed in advisory capacities
who participate in
the contract through their advisory function.
(1968) 262
CalApp.2d 222.) The very essence of section 1090 is predicated upon the
concept that public officials "are expected to exercise absolute loyalty and
undivided allegiance to the best itrterests of the governmental body or
agency...;'\atp.237.
Once it has been established that a public official is involved, the
next issue is whether a contract- has been made in his/her official capacity.
The first step of the inquiry is to be certain that a contract has been
finalized. If it is established that a public olficial with a financial interest
has participated in the making of the contract, a violation occurs.
tAIl fut I! alde citatioos will bc to thc Covem6edt Codc unlcss othcreisc spcrifically notcd.


Korade
March 13, 7992
The courts have defined the making of a contract to include
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasonhg, planning,
drawing of plans and specifications and solicitation of bids.
Association for Residential Survival v. Citv of Millbrae , spra, 262
Cal.App.2d. 222,237.
The public official must have a financial interest in the contract
which is being made. Section 1090 does not clearly define what constitutes
a financial interest, although several cases have interpreted the term
broadly. In addition, the kgislature has corlified certain remote interests
which are not deemed sufficient to rise to the level of being interested in
the contract. These remote interests are specific atrd must be disclosed to
the body on which the officer sits and noted in the official records (Gov.
Code $ 1091). Often times these "remote" interests disqualify the individual
under the Political Reform Act.
From the facts provided it is not altogether clear what the level of
the Commission's involvement is in the review of the Projects. Therefore,
the certainty of Mr. Wood's participation in the plaming for the Projects
or involvement leading up to employment of an architect is speculative.
However, assuming arguendo, Mr. Wood's duties on the Commission include
participatio4 negotiations, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, or
solicitation of bids, any subsequent actions which would result in his making
an agreement with the City to do the architectural work would violate
section 1090. Applyrng tle tlree-prong test, Mr. Wood was a member of
the commission, participated in the making and culmination of the contract,
and has a clear financial interest in the contract as he would receive
compensation for professional services.
If Mr. Wood's involvement is restricted to review of schematic or
conceptual plans for ttre Projects, the potential application of section 1090
is more remote. If the involvement of the Economic Development
Commission, includes recommending approval of the specifications for the
Projects, a reasonable nexus between the participation of Mr. Wood and his
subsequent ability to bid the project as an architect may be established. In
that instance, it is possible a section 1090 violation could occur.
If the Commission only reviews schematics and makes
recommendations on the overall design of the projects, then I believe
participation does not reach the level of a section i090 violation. The
second prong of the test, participation, in the making of a contract would
not be satisfied by these circumstances.


Korade
March 13, 1992
This opinion does not ad&ess other possibie violations of law, such
as the Political Reform Act. If you have any questions please do not
hesitate to call.
RJL/kl


City of Al:rrnerlu California

CONCLUSION: If the Commission's review of the Projects includes negotiation, planning,
drawing of any plans and specifications, or solicitation of bids for
architectural services, a contract with Robert Wood for architectural seryices
would violate section 1090 of the Government Code.

Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95814-0807 Re: Additional Information for Request Number 91-087 Dear Ms. Stecher: Enclosed please find the additional information we discussed: 1. A chart showing the projects of the Economic Development Commission which were forwarded to the City Council for action. This chart shows the recommendation of the Economic Development Commission and the action of the City Council. 2. A copy of a letter from an attorney discussing possible Government Code section 1090 implications. This letter may provided you with some additional background information. City staff believes, at this point, that the Economic Development Commission's involvement will be limited to reviewing the plans and making recommendations about the overall Civic Center Plans. For your information, the Economic Development Commission is currently listed as an advisory body in the City's Conflict of Interest Code. Please let me know if I can provide you with any additional information. We would appreciate your response as soon as possible. Very truly yours, Heather C.
